text: Bacterial wound infections have become a major clinical challenge due to the increasing prevalence of antibiotic resistance. In this study, we for the first time reported an antibiotic-free hybrid nanozyme for antimicrobial wound healing. Based on the method of combining metal-organic framework (MOF) with ultra-small Au nanoparticles (Au NPs), ultra-small Au NPs were successfully grown in situ on the surface of MOF-stabilized iron oxide (Fe3O4) nanoparticles (Fe3O4@MOF@Au NPs, FMA NPs). The as-prepared FMA NPs possessed synergistic peroxidase-like activities. Moreover, the generated hydroxyl radicals (·OH) in the presence of low dose of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 0.97 mM) were evaluated and the results confirmed that the FMA NPs exhibited not only biocompatibility but also favorable antibacterial activities against Gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia coli, E. coli) and Gram-positive bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus, S. aureus). Finally, by using animal experiment, the results showed that the hybrid nanozymes facilitated wound healing while with adequate biosafety. Overall, the designed hybrid nanozymes hold great promise for the treatment of bacterial wound infections.
explanation: The passage is rich in domain-specific details that resemble a real scientific abstract, but some phrasing choices (e.g., 'for the first time reported') and typical AI-generated clichés raise moderate suspicion of AI involvement.
